ontology.foodex2_description: Steamed bread / bun — wheat-based, filled, steamed
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code exists specifically for steamed pork bun; A0CKW (steamed bread/bun) is the closest defensible category anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for steamed pork bun as a specific food concept.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard steamed pork bun weighs approximately 80 g and is typically consumed as a single snack or dim sum serving.

nutrient_source.title: Steamed Pork Bun (鲜肉包) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for 鲜肉包 Steamed Pork Bun (cr_id G00010072). The HPB record reports 0g carbohydrate, 0g sugar, and 0g dietary fibre, which is inconsistent with the known composition of a wheat-flour bun. This anomaly is documented in notes. All values are preserved as reported by HPB; carbohydrate_g zero is flagged as a data integrity concern. Saturated fat not reported by HPB; set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; steamed white bread / bao analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal (peer-reviewed)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for 鲜肉包 (steamed pork bun) in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 65 is estimated from the closest analogous food: steamed white bread / plain baozi (GI ~65, medium category) as reported in Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). The HPB source records 0g carbohydrate for this item, which is inconsistent with the wheat-flour bun wrapper; glycemic load per serving is therefore set to 0.0 as a conservative placeholder pending correction of the carbohydrate data. If carbohydrate is corrected to ~25g per 80g serving, GL would be approximately 13.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 1050 mg/100g — far exceeds the 600 mg/100g upper threshold; soy sauce and oyster sauce in the filling are primary contributors.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit steamed pork buns due to sodium content of 1050 mg/100g; if consumed, restrict to half a bun and request reduced-sodium seasoning in the filling.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Wheat flour bun wrapper is a refined carbohydrate with estimated GI ~65 (medium-high); actual carbohydrate content is likely 40–50g/100g despite the HPB record showing 0g, posing a significant postprandial glucose risk.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should limit steamed pork buns to one small bun per sitting, pair with non-starchy vegetables to blunt glycaemic response, and request wholemeal flour buns where available.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium (1050 mg/100g) elevates blood pressure risk; pork filling contributes saturated fat and cholesterol (89 mg/100g), both of which adversely affect lipid profiles.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should minimise consumption of steamed pork buns due to combined high sodium and cholesterol load; if consumed, limit to one bun and choose lean pork filling preparations with reduced soy sauce.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sodium at 1050 mg/100g exceeds RA threshold; pork filling contributes arachidonic acid (omega-6) which amplifies TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory pathways; refined wheat flour wrapper increases glycaemic load driving systemic inflammation.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or strictly limit steamed pork buns; consider substituting with chicken or tofu filling and wholemeal bun wrappers to reduce inflammatory load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Steamed Pork Bun (鲜肉包, Xiān ròu bāo) is a classic Chinese dim sum and street food item widely consumed in Singapore and across the Chinese diaspora. It consists of a soft, fluffy steamed wheat-flour bun encasing a savoury filling of minced or chopped fresh pork seasoned with soy sauce, oyster sauce, sesame oil, ginger, and spring onion. The bun wrapper is made from leavened wheat dough that is steamed rather than baked, yielding a pillowy, slightly chewy texture with a moist interior. The filling is juicy and umami-rich, with a mild sweetness from the oyster sauce balanced by the saltiness of soy sauce. Steamed pork buns are commonly served at dim sum restaurants, hawker centres, and convenience stores throughout Singapore, and are popular as a breakfast, snack, or light meal item. From a nutritional standpoint, the HPB Singapore database records 171 kcal/100g, 19.1g protein, and 10.5g fat, with notably high sodium at 1050 mg/100g — a significant clinical concern for individuals with hypertension, cardiovascular disease, and rheumatoid arthritis. The recorded 0g carbohydrate is inconsistent with the known wheat-flour composition of the bun wrapper, which typically contributes 40–50g carbohydrate per 100g; this data anomaly is flagged for review. Cholesterol is 89 mg/100g. The glycaemic index is estimated at approximately 65 (medium) based on analogous steamed white bread data. Clinically, the high sodium content is the primary concern, followed by refined carbohydrate load from the bun wrapper and saturated fat from the pork filling. Contains gluten and soy allergens; not suitable for halal or vegetarian diets.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010072), which is the primary approved source for Singaporean dishes. Confidence is high for energy, protein, fat, sodium, and micronutrients as reported. The 0g carbohydrate anomaly is documented but does not reduce source confidence — it is a known data quality issue in the HPB record that requires upstream correction.
notes: ZERO-MACRO JUSTIFICATION (g4): carbohydrate_g is recorded as 0.0 and sugar_g as 0.0 and dietary_fibre_g as 0.0 as reported verbatim by HPB Singapore Foodo (cr_id G00010072). These values are NOT naturally zero — the wheat-flour bun wrapper typically contributes 40–50g carbohydrate/100g, 1–3g dietary fibre/100g, and 2–5g sugar/100g. The zero values represent a known data integrity anomaly in the HPB source record. They are preserved here as reported ('confirmed zero per HPB source') but are flagged for upstream correction. Users and clinicians should treat carbohydrate content as approximately 40–50g/100g for dietary planning purposes. NEGATIVE SENTINEL VALUES (g3): saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, vitamin panel, and moisture data for this steamed pork bun preparation are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry (cr_id G00010072) and are not available in USDA FoodData Central for an exact match as of 2026. GI NOTE: Glycemic load per serving is set to 0.0 as a conservative placeholder because the HPB source records 0g carbohydrate; if carbohydrate is corrected to ~25g per 80g serving, GL would be approximately 13 (medium). ONTOLOGY NOTE: FoodEx2 code A0CKW is used as the closest defensible category anchor for steamed filled bun; no exact FoodEx2 code exists for steamed pork bun specifically.
